segments.title: Why medieval sea battles resembled land battles
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke connects castle engineering to medieval naval combat, where fighting at sea often worked like a land battle transferred from one ship to another.
segments.body: Castles concentrate engineering and architecture so a small defending force can resist a much larger attacker. Dr. Clarke uses that idea to explain his interest in comparing land and sea warfare, observing that medieval naval engagements often became close combat fought across ships. This brief passage is a useful watch point for understanding how boarding-era tactics blurred the boundary between battles ashore and battles afloat.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke explains castle defensive engineering and compares medieval sea battles with land battles fought from ship to ship.
